summary:
The Kilwa Sultanate (c. 960–1513 AD) was the dominant commercial power of the East African Swahili coast, centered on the island of Kilwa Kisiwani (in modern Tanzania), and the most prosperous and powerful of the city-states that made up the medieval [[event:2178|Swahili Coast]] trading network connecting sub-Saharan Africa with the Indian Ocean commercial world. Founded according to tradition by a Persian prince who purchased the island from local inhabitants, Kilwa grew wealthy by controlling the export of gold from the interior — particularly from the Zimbabwe Plateau mines that also supported [[event:2177|Great Zimbabwe]] — and by taxing the extensive trade in ivory, slaves, iron, and copper.

Kilwa's wealth at its peak in the 13th–14th centuries was sufficient to impress [[event:2285|Ibn Battuta]], who visited around 1331 and described it as 'one of the most beautiful and well-constructed cities in the world,' with mosques, palaces, and merchants' houses built in coral stone. The Great Mosque of Kilwa — the largest medieval mosque in sub-Saharan Africa — and the Husuni Kubwa palace complex (with its octagonal swimming pool and elaborate warehouse system for trade goods) survive as archaeological sites demonstrating the sophistication of Swahili urban civilization. Kilwa's monopoly on the Sofala-Zimbabwe gold trade made it fabulously wealthy but also dependent on a single commodity: when the Portuguese under Pedro Álvares Cabral's fleet arrived in 1500 and Vasco da Gama returned in 1502, demanding tribute and access, Kilwa's commercial dominance collapsed rapidly under European naval pressure.
